Jesus enters the temple and teaches with an authority that provokes a dangerous question: By what authority do you do these things? The religious leaders who ask it are not genuinely seeking understanding—they are testing Him, probing for grounds to condemn Him. When Jesus responds with His own question about John's baptism, He exposes their spiritual bankruptcy. These men who claim to know God's will cannot even discern whether John came from Heaven. They calculate their answer based on what the crowd will accept, not what is true. Their fear of the people becomes the standard by which they measure truth. Jesus refuses to answer them on their terms. The authority question remains unresolved—not because Jesus lacks authority, but because those asking have already decided they will reject whatever He claims.

But Jesus does not simply walk away. He tells the parable of the vineyard tenants, and what begins as a story becomes a confrontation with reality itself. The vineyard is God's own people. The tenants are those entrusted with God's care. Servant after servant comes—the prophets—and they are rejected, beaten, shamefully treated. Then comes the beloved son. The tenants see Him and know exactly who He is: the heir. And in that moment of clarity, they choose murder. They will kill the Son and seize the inheritance. It is a portrait of human rebellion so stark, so deliberate, that the crowd listening recoils: "May such a thing never happen!" But Jesus looks at them—directly, unflinchingly—and speaks the words of Scripture: "The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone." This is not comfort. This is the announcement that their rejection of Christ will not prevent His triumph. Those who fall on this stone will be broken. Those on whom it falls will be crushed. Authority does not come from the permission of the powerful. It comes from God, and it stands unshaken.

The officials understand. They want to arrest Jesus immediately, but fear of the crowd holds them back, so they shift to a different strategy. They send spies who flatter Him—"we know you teach correctly"—while laying traps with poisoned questions. On taxes, on resurrection, on the nature of the Messiah, they come again and again. Each time, Jesus cuts through their game. With the Sadducees, who deny resurrection, He does not merely answer their riddle about the widow married to seven brothers; He reorients their entire understanding of God's nature. The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is not the God of the dead but of the living. All are alive to Him. In God's sight, resurrection is not an impossible complication—it is the natural order of His kingdom. Even as the religious leaders scheme, even as they speak with false reverence and genuine malice, Jesus speaks truth that unmasks them and expands the horizons of what His followers should believe.

Then comes the final reversal. Jesus Himself poses the question: How can the Messiah be David's son when David himself calls Him Lord? The authorities have no answer. And with the temple listening, Jesus turns to His disciples and teaches them to see through the scribes entirely. Their long robes, their love of honor, their prayers for show—these are not the costumes of faithfulness. They devour widows' houses and will receive greater condemnation. In this chapter, authority is revealed. It is not seized through politics or flattery or careful calculation. It belongs to the One who speaks truth that shatters human schemes, who rises when He is rejected, who sees through every disguise. The question of who gives authority has been answered, not in words but in an action no amount of plotting can stop.
